title: ACT replaces the legacy deriveShareKey, which used a publicly derivable SHA-256 of both addresses.
content:
## Vulnerability (fixed)

The original document-sharing code derived its encryption key as:

```
key = SHA-256(sender_address + ":" + recipient_address)
```

Both inputs are public (on-chain, ENS, etherscan). Any third party who knew both addresses could reconstruct the key and decrypt any shared bundle. This was **security theater**, not encryption.

## Fix: Swarm ACT (Access Control Trie)

ACT derives its session key via ECDH:

```
session_key = SHA-256( ECDH(publisher_privkey, grantee_pubkey) || salt )
```

The publisher's **private key** is required — only their Bee node has it. A third party with both public keys still faces the discrete-log problem.

## Comparison

| | Old (deriveShareKey) | New (ACT) |
|---|---|---|
| Key derivation | `SHA-256(public_addr_a + public_addr_b)` | `ECDH(privkey × pubkey) + salt` |
| Third-party attack | Trivial | Impossible — DLP |
| Encryption | App-level AES-256-GCM | Bee node native |
| Grantee management | None | `patchGrantees()` add/remove |
| Revocation | Impossible | Rebuild ACT with new access key |

## Status

ShareManager rewritten, 105/105 tests passing. ACT now protects document sharing, chat history feeds, and multi-party collaboration data.
description: Old share encryption hashed sender_address + recipient_address — both public values — so any third party could decrypt. ACT uses ECDH requiring the sender's private key.
